perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each party and its respective directors, officers, stockholders, affiliates, and agents, and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each party and its respective directors, officers, stockholders, affiliates, and agents.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the governing law clause, the contract becomes ambiguous regarding which state's laws apply. While it might default to New York based on where the contract was signed, removing the explicit clause opens it to interpretation and potential challenges, especially regarding consumer protection laws if one party is based elsewhere.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the governing law clause creates ambiguity, and may conflict with NY GBL § 399-c that mandates clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions, including governing law, especially in consumer contracts.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the governing law clause in the changed text creates ambiguity, potentially conflicting with NY GBL § 399-c's implied need for clear terms. Scraped Law Snippet 1, although it doesn't directly state 399-c, points to the General Business Article 26, which contains sections like 399-BBB and 399-CC that mandate disclosures. Omitting the governing law makes the contract's terms less clear, increasing the risk of violating disclosure rules under New York law. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition as it removes essential information related to legal jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that neither party nor any of its Representatives makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material made available by it or to it. Each party agrees that neither party nor any of its Representatives shall have any liability to the other party or to any of its Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of such other party's Evaluation Material or any errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that neither party nor any of its Representatives makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material made available by it or to it.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the liability disclaimer regarding errors and omissions in the Evaluation Material, the contract becomes less protective of the parties involved. While parties are generally responsible for their own due diligence, this clause clarifies the absence of liability for unintentional errors. Its removal could lead to disputes and potential claims, especially concerning financial or business projections.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the explicit disclaimer of warranties and liability, especially regarding the accuracy of information provided, may conflict with the requirements of NY UCC § 2-316 for clear and conspicuous disclaimers, potentially creating implied warranties where none were intended, concerning the sale of goods or information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-315 Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-317 Cumulation and Conflict of Warranties Express or Implied This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-316 Exclusion or Modification of Warranties Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2--202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof. " (3) Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remedy (Sections 2--718 and 2--719).
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a liability disclaimer, specifically concerning errors and omissions in the Evaluation Material. The Law Explanation suggests this conflicts with NY UCC § 2-316, potentially creating unintended implied warranties. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 2-316, addresses the 'Exclusion or Modification of Warranties', stating conditions under which warranties can be excluded. The original text explicitly excluded liability for errors or omissions. By removing this exclusion, the changed text arguably fails to clearly disclaim potential warranties, creating a legal contradiction because it could be interpreted as not meeting the requirements for warranty exclusion under UCC 2-316, particularly regarding clear and conspicuous disclaimers.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a party or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the other party's Evaluation Material, the party requested or required to make the disclosure shall provide the other party with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a party or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the other party's Evaluation Material, the party requested or required to make the disclosure shall provide the other party with notice of any such request.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes the word 'prompt' and the subsequent clause about the other party's right to seek a protective order or waive compliance. Removing the other party's right to seek a protective order may impede its ability to safeguard confidential information as protected by law. This can result in the disclosure of private information that is legally protected, possibly exposing the company to legal action.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3103
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the provision that allows a party to seek a protective order, the modified text contradicts the spirit of NY CPLR § 3103, which allows courts to issue protective orders to prevent unreasonable annoyance, expense, embarrassment, disadvantage, or other prejudice to any person or the courts.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Law § 3103 (2022) - Protective Orders. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 31 - Disclosure 3103 - Protective Orders. Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 3103 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 3103. Protective orders. (a) Prevention of abuse. The court may at any time on its own initiative, or on motion of any party or of any person from whom or about whom discovery is sought, make a protective order denying, limiting, conditioning or regulating the use of any disclosure device. Such order shall be designed to prevent unreasonable annoyance, expense, embarrassment, disadvantage, or other prejudice to any person or the courts. (b) Suspension of disclosure pending application for protective order. Service of a notice of motion for a protective order shall suspend disclosure of the particular matter in dispute. (c) Suppression of information improperly obtained. If any disclosure under this article has been improperly or irregularly obtained so that a substantial right of a party is prejudiced, the court, on motion, may make an appropriate order, including an order that the information be suppressed. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'prompt' notification requirement and the clause about seeking a protective order or waiving compliance, potentially contradicting NY CPLR § 3103, which allows courts to issue protective orders to prevent prejudice. The original text ensures the other party has time to seek such protection. The omission impedes the ability to safeguard confidential information and seek legal remedies, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, NY CPLR § 3103, confirms the court's ability to issue protective orders to prevent 'unreasonable annoyance, expense, embarrassment, disadvantage, or other prejudice,' which the original text facilitated but the modified text weakens.
